final_answer: Secure the WebSocket channel by always using wss (TLS) to encrypt traffic (N1:0) and verify the Origin header (N5:3). Authenticate each upgrade with a short‑lived signed JWT (or similar token) that includes a per‑connection nonce or jti claim (N1:1, N5:4) and rotate or invalidate the token on reconnect (N1:7). Protect every message with a per‑message HMAC or signed payload (N1:6) so tampering and replay of old messages are detectable. Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in each event per client (N1:2) and have the server keep the highest sequence number seen, discarding any event with a lower or equal number (N1:3). Additionally, attach a globally unique event ID (UUID) and maintain a short‑term cache (e.g., LRU) of recent IDs to drop duplicate retransmissions (N1:4). Define a replay window based on recent sequence numbers or timestamps and ignore events outside this window (N3:2). When a client reconnects, it should send the last processed sequence number so the server can resume from the next number (N3:3). Optionally, use timestamps to buffer out‑of‑order events for a limited time before discarding them (N1:5). Implement a sliding window of accepted nonces/timestamps to further guard against replay attacks (N5:6).
